question: What safety enhancement does Table 1.2-2 attribute to the integral RCS, and how does Figure 5.1-2 illustrate this design?

nuscale_ch01_0118.title: 1.8 Interfaces with Certified Design
nuscale_ch01_0118.summary: This section describes the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant’s certified design and the site-specific design provided in the combined license (COL) application. It explains the types of interface requirements (conceptual design information and COL information items), identifies where these interfaces and information items are documented, and notes that COL applicants must address any departures from the certified design.

nuscale_ch01_0072.title: Combined License Information Items
nuscale_ch01_0072.summary: This section explains the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant’s certified design and the site-specific design elements provided in the Combined License (COL) application. It distinguishes between conceptual design information (CDI) for non-certified plant portions and COL information items, which are site-specific responsibilities of the COL applicant. The section also notes that COL applicants must provide a list of any departures from the certified design.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located within the containment vessel and includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. The RCS removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and features systems for degasification and emergency core cooling to maintain safety and integrity during both normal and emergency operations.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator, ensuring safe and efficient operation. The RCS design supports up to twelve independent modules, maintains proper coolant chemistry, and ensures uniform boron concentration to protect fuel integrity during all operating conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section describes the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS), which uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to ensure safe and efficient cooling, maintain water chemistry, and provide uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information. It references tables and figures that list and explain the symbols used in these diagrams, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by COL applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch01_0020.title: Safety Considerations
nuscale_ch01_0020.summary: The NuScale design enhances safety by maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), which reduces corrosion, aids leak detection, and limits combustible gases. Its passive safety systems and integral design eliminate the need for external power and large coolant loops, significantly lowering the risk of core damage compared to traditional nuclear plants.

nuscale_ch01_0022.title: Engineered Safety Feature Materials
nuscale_ch01_0022.summary: The NuScale reactor’s containment vessel (CNV) is maintained under vacuum during normal operation, which reduces corrosion, aids in leak detection, minimizes heat transfer, and limits oxygen for safety. The design relies on passive safety features—such as natural cooling and elimination of external coolant loops—which lower the risk of core damage and reduce radioactive inventory compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0051.title: Plant Features of Special Interest
nuscale_ch01_0051.summary: This section describes key plant features of special interest in the NuScale Power Plant, including the Central Utility Building (CUB) and Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs), which house essential but nonsafety-related systems and backup power equipment. It also highlights human factors considerations, such as fail-safe design, advanced automation, and digital controls, aimed at minimizing human error and optimizing operator workload, in compliance with regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0067.title: Drawings and Other Detailed Information
nuscale_ch01_0067.summary: This section describes the inclusion of simplified instrumentation, control, electrical, and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information alongside written descriptions. It lists the types of diagrams provided, references legends for interpreting symbols, and notes that applicants referencing the NuScale design must supply site-specific diagrams and legends as needed.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section describes the testing and inspection requirements for the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) components, including compliance with ASME codes for preservice and inservice inspections. It details how DHRS actuation valves are exercised and tested, primarily during cold shutdown conditions, and outlines the procedures for verifying valve function and position, including a one-time in-situ test for the first reactor module during initial startup.

nuscale_ch01_0863.title: Table 1.9-8: Conformance with SECY-93-087, "Policy, Technical, and Licensing Issues Pertaining to Evolutionary and Advanced Light-Water Reactor Designs"
nuscale_ch01_0863.summary: Table 1.9-8 summarizes NuScale’s conformance with SECY-93-087 policy and technical issues for advanced light-water reactor designs. The table lists each issue, NuScale’s conformance status (conforms, partially conforms, or not applicable), and relevant comments or references to supporting sections in the safety analysis report. This demonstrates how NuScale addresses regulatory expectations for safety and design features.

nuscale_ch01_0044.title: Chemical and Volume Control System
nuscale_ch01_0044.summary: The Chemical and Volume Control System section describes the NuScale reactor’s passive safety features, including two independent decay heat removal trains, each capable of fully cooling the reactor core. In the event of an accident or loss of power, heat is transferred from the reactor to a large, stainless steel-lined pool (the ultimate heat sink) via natural circulation, ensuring continued cooling without external power or active systems.

nuscale_ch01_0019.title: Operating Characteristics
nuscale_ch01_0019.summary: The NuScale Power Module (NPM) operates using natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps, and features a small, modular, integral pressurized water reactor design. It employs passive safety systems—including dedicated emergency core cooling and decay heat removal systems—that do not rely on electrical power, and its components are housed in steel containment partially immersed in water for effective heat removal. The NPM allows for flexible power maneuvering using control rods, soluble boron, or both, and is designed for modular fabrication and enhanced safety.
